title: Mariosousa durangensis
what_description: Mariosousa durangensis is a species of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae, native to Mexico. The plant is a tree or shrub, reaching 5-10 meters in height, with a spread of 2-4 meters, and a trunk diameter of 10-30 cm. Its leaves are compound, composed of 2-4 pairs of leaflets, each 2-5 cm long, with a dark green color and a glossy texture. The inflorescence is a spike of 5-10 flowers, each 2-4 cm long, with a yellow or white color, and a sweet fragrance. The fruit is a flat pod, 5-10 cm long, containing 2-4 seeds, each 1-2 cm long.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Mariosousa durangensis are inflammation, diabetes, and hypertension, which are alleviated by its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and flavonoid properties. Mariosousa durangensis also helps to prevent cancer, particularly colon and breast cancer, due to its high content of flavonoids and phenolic acids. Additionally, it has been shown to treat digestive issues such as gastritis and gastroenteritis, as well as skin irritation and skin infections caused by its antibacterial and antifungal properties. Furthermore, it has been used to alleviate cardiovascular disease, including high cholesterol, due to its ability to lower LDL levels and increase HDL levels. Mariosousa durangensis also exhibits anti-cancer properties, reduces fever, alleviates chronic pain, rheumatism, and anxiety, and helps to manage asthma and diarrhea.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Mariosousa durangensis are rooted in its antioxidant activity, which involves the scavenging of free radicals and the protection of cells from oxidative damage, while its antimicrobial properties allow it to combat a wide range of microorganisms, including bacteria and fungi, by inhibiting their growth and reproduction, and its anti-inflammatory properties enable it to reduce inflammation and alleviate associated pain, in addition to its analgesic properties, which allow it to relieve pain by blocking the transmission of pain signals, and finally, it also exhibits antifungal properties, which enable it to inhibit the growth of fungi and prevent the spread of fungal infections.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Mariosousa durangensis are quercetin, kaempferol, isorhapontigenin, flavonoids, and phenolic acids. These compounds have been identified as the primary bioactive constituents responsible for the medicinal properties of Mariosousa durangensis. Quercetin and kaempferol are flavonoids with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, which contribute to the plant's potential in reducing inflammation and oxidative stress. Isorhapontigenin is a phenolic compound with neuroprotective properties, suggesting its potential in preventing neurodegenerative diseases. The flavonoids and phenolic acids in Mariosousa durangensis may also contribute to its antimicrobial and anti-cancer properties.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Mariosousa durangensis are derived from its medicinal properties, which include antifungal, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory activities. A tea can be prepared by infusing the leaves of Mariosousa durangensis in hot water, while a decoction is made by boiling the roots or stems in water to extract its active compounds. A tincture is a concentrated liquid extract of the plant, obtained by macerating the leaves or stems in a solvent like ethanol or glycerin. A salve is a topical preparation made by mixing the tincture or infusion with a carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, and is used to treat skin conditions. An infusion, similar to tea, is also used to make a liquid extract of the plant, and is often used in traditional medicine to treat various health conditions.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Mariosousa durangensis improperly are headaches, nausea, diarrhea, and skin irritation. These adverse reactions can occur due to the plant's high alkaloid content, specifically saponins and glycosides, which can cause gastrointestinal upset and skin irritation. The improper use of Mariosousa durangensis may lead to an imbalance of these compounds, resulting in headaches and nausea. In severe cases, prolonged exposure to the plant's saponins can cause diarrhea and exacerbate existing gastrointestinal conditions. Furthermore, the plant's glycosides may cause skin irritation, particularly in individuals with sensitive skin, leading to redness, itching, and discomfort.

It is a part of the genus Mariosousa, which is a group of plants native to the Americas.
